prompt: Give the truth table for ¬A (negation).
solution: If A is true (T) then ¬A is false (F); if A is false (F) then ¬A is true (T).

prompt: Give the truth table for A ∧ B (conjunction).
solution: A ∧ B is true only when both A and B are true. Rows: (T,T)→T, (T,F)→F, (F,T)→F, (F,F)→F.

prompt: Give the truth table for A ∨ B (disjunction).
solution: A ∨ B is true when at least one of A or B is true. Rows: (T,T)→T, (T,F)→T, (F,T)→T, (F,F)→F.

prompt: Give the truth table for A → B (implication) and explain the truth of the case A=F, B=T and A=F, B=F.
solution: A → B is false only when A is true and B is false. Rows: (T,T)→T, (T,F)→F, (F,T)→T, (F,F)→T. When A is false the implication is true (vacuously).

prompt: Give the truth table for A ↔ B (biconditional).
solution: A ↔ B is true exactly when A and B have the same truth value. Rows: (T,T)→T, (T,F)→F, (F,T)→F, (F,F)→T.

prompt: State and apply De Morgan's laws for ¬(A ∧ B) and ¬(A ∨ B).
solution: ¬(A ∧ B) ≡ ¬A ∨ ¬B and ¬(A ∨ B) ≡ ¬A ∧ ¬B. To apply, push negation inward turning conjunctions into disjunctions and vice versa.

prompt: Convert (A ∨ (B ∧ C)) to an equivalent formula in conjunctive normal form (CNF).
solution: Distribute ∨ over ∧: A ∨ (B ∧ C) ≡ (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ C). This is the CNF.

prompt: Explain the notions: tautology, contradiction, satisfiable, and logically equivalent.
solution: Tautology: true under every valuation. Contradiction: false under every valuation. Satisfiable: true under at least one valuation. Logically equivalent: two formulas have identical truth values under every valuation.

prompt: Show that ¬(A → B) is logically equivalent to A ∧ ¬B.
solution: A → B ≡ ¬A ∨ B, so ¬(A → B) ≡ ¬(¬A ∨ B) ≡ A ∧ ¬B by De Morgan.

prompt: Translate the English sentence "If it rains then the ground is wet" into propositional logic and show when it is false.
solution: Let R = "it rains", W = "ground is wet". Formula: R → W. It is false exactly when R is true and W is false (it rains but ground is not wet).

prompt: Give the natural deduction introduction and elimination rules for ∧ (and).
solution: ∧-Introduction: from A and B infer A ∧ B. ∧-Elimination: from A ∧ B infer A; and from A ∧ B infer B.

prompt: State a simple method to test whether a propositional formula is satisfiable using truth tables.
solution: List all valuations of propositional variables, evaluate the formula on each row; if any row yields true, the formula is satisfiable. If none do, it's unsatisfiable (a contradiction).

prompt: Give the truth table for A NAND B (A ↑ B).
solution: A ↑ B is true except when both A and B are true. Rows: (T,T)→F, (T,F)→T, (F,T)→T, (F,F)→T.

prompt: Give the truth table for A NOR B (A ↓ B).
solution: A ↓ B is true only when both A and B are false. Rows: (T,T)→F, (T,F)→F, (F,T)→F, (F,F)→T.

prompt: Give the truth table for A XOR B (exclusive or).
solution: A ⊕ B is true when exactly one of A or B is true. Rows: (T,T)→F, (T,F)→T, (F,T)→T, (F,F)→F.

prompt: Give the truth table for A XNOR B (equivalence by negated XOR).
solution: XNOR is true when A and B have the same truth value. Rows: (T,T)→T, (T,F)→F, (F,T)→F, (F,F)→T.

prompt: Explain the contrapositive equivalence of implication.
solution: A → B is logically equivalent to ¬B → ¬A (the contrapositive); they have the same truth table.

prompt: State and apply double negation.
solution: Double negation: ¬¬A ≡ A. You can eliminate or introduce two negations without changing meaning.

prompt: State the commutative laws for ∧ and ∨.
solution: Commutative: A ∧ B ≡ B ∧ A and A ∨ B ≡ B ∨ A; order of operands does not matter.

prompt: State the associative laws for ∧ and ∨.
solution: Associative: (A ∧ B) ∧ C ≡ A ∧ (B ∧ C) and (A ∨ B) ∨ C ≡ A ∨ (B ∨ C); grouping does not matter.

prompt: State the distributive laws in propositional logic.
solution: Distributive: A ∧ (B ∨ C) ≡ (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C) and A ∨ (B ∧ C) ≡ (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ C).

prompt: State the absorption laws and give an example.
solution: Absorption: A ∨ (A ∧ B) ≡ A and A ∧ (A ∨ B) ≡ A; e.g., A ∨ (A ∧ B) simplifies to A.

prompt: State idempotent laws for ∧ and ∨.
solution: Idempotent: A ∧ A ≡ A and A ∨ A ≡ A; repeating the same operand has no effect.

prompt: Convert A ∧ (B ∨ C) to DNF.
solution: Distribute ∧ over ∨: A ∧ (B ∨ C) ≡ (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C), which is a DNF.

prompt: Define CNF and DNF informally.
solution: CNF: conjunction of clauses where each clause is a disjunction of literals. DNF: disjunction of terms where each term is a conjunction of literals.

prompt: Explain the resolution rule with a short example.
solution: Resolution: from (A ∨ B) and (¬A ∨ C) infer (B ∨ C). Example: (P ∨ Q) and (¬P ∨ R) resolve to (Q ∨ R).

prompt: What is unit resolution (unit propagation)?
solution: Unit resolution: when a clause is a single literal L, remove ¬L from other clauses or simplify clauses accordingly, propagating consequences.

prompt: Give a simple satisfiable formula example and a satisfying valuation.
solution: Formula: A ∨ (¬A ∧ B) is satisfiable; valuation A = T satisfies it (A ∨ ...) = T.

prompt: Give a simple unsatisfiable (contradiction) formula.
solution: A ∧ ¬A is unsatisfiable because no valuation makes both A and ¬A true simultaneously.

prompt: Define semantic entailment (logical consequence).
solution: Γ ⊨ φ means every valuation that makes all formulas in Γ true also makes φ true.

prompt: Define syntactic derivability.
solution: Γ ⊢ φ means φ can be derived from premises Γ using a formal proof system (rules of inference).

prompt: State the soundness theorem informally.
solution: Soundness: if Γ ⊢ φ then Γ ⊨ φ; derivable statements are semantically valid consequences.

prompt: State the completeness theorem informally (for propositional logic).
solution: Completeness: if Γ ⊨ φ then Γ ⊢ φ; every semantic consequence has a formal proof.

prompt: State and illustrate Modus Ponens.
solution: Modus Ponens: from A and A → B infer B. Example: A, A → B ⊢ B.

prompt: State and illustrate Modus Tollens.
solution: Modus Tollens: from A → B and ¬B infer ¬A. Example: if 'If it rains then wet' and not wet, infer not raining.

prompt: Explain the rule of substitution for logical equivalence.
solution: If φ ≡ ψ then any occurrence of φ in a formula can be replaced by ψ preserving logical equivalence of the whole formula.

prompt: What is the Tseitin transformation (brief)?
solution: Tseitin: introduce fresh variables for subformulas to produce an equisatisfiable CNF of linear size, useful for SAT encoding.

prompt: Convert ¬(A ∨ (¬B ∧ C)) into CNF stepwise.
solution: Push negation: ¬A ∧ ¬(¬B ∧ C) ≡ ¬A ∧ (B ∨ ¬C). This is already in CNF: (¬A) ∧ (B ∨ ¬C).

prompt: Convert (A ∨ B) ∧ C into DNF.
solution: Distribute: (A ∨ B) ∧ C ≡ (A ∧ C) ∨ (B ∧ C), a DNF.

prompt: Show implication as a disjunction.
solution: A → B is equivalent to ¬A ∨ B; use this to transform implications into propositional connectives.

prompt: Explain vacuous truth for implications.
solution: An implication A → B is true when A is false regardless of B; such cases are called vacuously true.

prompt: Clarify material equivalence vs biconditional.
solution: Material equivalence A ↔ B is the biconditional connective; it is true exactly when A and B share truth value (equivalent).

prompt: Define literal, clause, and Horn clause.
solution: Literal: atom or its negation. Clause: disjunction of literals. Horn clause: clause with at most one positive literal.

prompt: Why are Horn-SAT instances special?
solution: Satisfiability of Horn clauses can be decided in linear time using forward chaining; easier than general SAT.

prompt: Give a formal definition of CNF.
solution: CNF is a conjunction of one or more clauses, where each clause is a disjunction of literals.

prompt: Give a formal definition of DNF.
solution: DNF is a disjunction of one or more terms, where each term is a conjunction of literals.

prompt: State the ¬-introduction rule (proof by contradiction) in natural deduction.
solution: ¬-Introduction: assume A, derive a contradiction ⊥, then infer ¬A discharging the assumption.

prompt: State the ¬-elimination (ex falso) rule.
solution: From a contradiction (⊥) you may infer any formula B (ex falso quodlibet).

prompt: Explain the principle of explosion (ex falso quodlibet).
solution: If a contradiction holds, then any formula can be derived; inconsistency trivializes a theory.

prompt: Give an example of a proof by contrapositive.
solution: To prove A → B, prove ¬B → ¬A instead; e.g., to show 'if n is even then n^2 is even' contrapositive works similarly.

prompt: Prove A→C from A→B and B→C (hypothetical syllogism).
solution: Assume A; from A and A→B infer B; from B and B→C infer C; discharge assumption to conclude A→C.

prompt: State the relation (A→B) ∧ (B→C) ⇒ A→C succinctly.
solution: If A implies B and B implies C, then A implies C; this is transitivity of implication.

prompt: Describe the semantic tableau (truth tree) method briefly.
solution: Build a tree by decomposing formulas according to rules; a closed tree (all branches contradiction) shows unsatisfiability.

prompt: Use a small truth tree to show {A→B, A, ¬B} is inconsistent.
solution: Start with A→B, A, ¬B: decompose A→B to ¬A ∨ B; branch gives B which contradicts ¬B; every branch closes, so inconsistent.

prompt: Briefly describe the DPLL algorithm.
solution: DPLL: backtracking SAT solver using unit propagation, pure literal elimination, and splitting to search for satisfying assignments.

prompt: What is pure literal elimination?
solution: If a literal appears with only one polarity (always positive or always negative), set it to satisfy its clauses and simplify.

prompt: Define a pure literal with an example.
solution: Pure literal: appears only as P (never ¬P) or only as ¬P; e.g., in clauses {P ∨ Q, P ∨ R}, P is pure.

prompt: Explain backtracking in SAT solving.
solution: Backtracking chooses a variable assignment, propagates consequences, and if contradiction occurs, reverts and tries opposite assignment.

prompt: State briefly why SAT is NP-complete.
solution: Cook-Levin theorem: SAT encodes nondeterministic polynomial computations; every NP problem reduces to SAT in polynomial time.

prompt: Simplify using absorption: A ∨ (A ∧ B) ≡ ?
solution: A ∨ (A ∧ B) ≡ A by absorption; the larger expression adds nothing beyond A.

prompt: Simplify using idempotence: A ∨ A ≡ ?
solution: A ∨ A ≡ A; duplicates of the same literal collapse.

prompt: Show the currying equivalence: (A ∧ B) → C ≡ A → (B → C).
Convert ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C)) into DNF.
solution: ¬(A ∧ (B ∨ C)) ≡ ¬A ∨ ¬(B ∨ C) ≡ ¬A ∨ (¬B ∧ ¬C), which is a DNF (disjunction of terms).

prompt: Give a CNF for A ↔ B.
solution: A ↔ B ≡ (A → B) ∧ (B → A) ≡ (¬A ∨ B) ∧ (¬B ∨ A), already in CNF.

prompt: Simplify (A ∨ B) ∧ (A ∨ ¬B).
solution: Distribute: ≡ A ∨ (B ∧ ¬B) ≡ A ∨ false ≡ A, since B ∧ ¬B is false.

prompt: Demonstrate unit propagation on clauses {A ∨ B, ¬A, ¬B ∨ C}.
solution: ¬A makes A ∨ B become B (unit). B makes ¬B ∨ C become C (unit). Propagation sets B=true, C=true.

prompt: Give a pure literal elimination example.
solution: Clauses {P ∨ Q, P ∨ R}: P appears only positively (pure), set P=true and simplify clauses (both satisfied).

prompt: Perform a Tseitin transformation for A ∨ (B ∧ C).
solution: Introduce X for (B ∧ C) and Y for A ∨ X. Clauses: (¬X ∨ B), (¬X ∨ C), (X ∨ ¬B ∨ ¬C) for X↔B∧C; (¬Y ∨ A ∨ X), (Y ∨ ¬A), (Y ∨ ¬X) for Y↔A∨X. Conjoin all in CNF.

prompt: Define a prime implicant with a short example.
solution: Prime implicant: a minimal conjunction of literals implying the formula. For F = (A ∧ B) ∨ (A ∧ C), A∧B and A∧C are prime implicants.

prompt: Give the minimal models of A ∨ (B ∧ C).
solution: Models where formula true with no proper subset true: {A} and {B,C} are minimal satisfying sets of variables.

prompt: Resolve clauses {A ∨ B, ¬A, ¬B} to show inconsistency.
solution: Resolve A ∨ B with ¬A to get B. B with ¬B yields empty clause ⊥. Branch closes, set inconsistent.

prompt: Use proof by contradiction to derive ¬A from A → ⊥ and ⊥→? example.
solution: If A → ⊥ (i.e. A implies contradiction), assume A then derive ⊥, discharge assumption to infer ¬A by ¬-introduction.

prompt: Define implicate and prime implicate with an example.
solution: Implicate: a clause entailed by F. Prime implicate: minimal implicate. For F=A∨B the clause A∨B is a prime implicate.

prompt: Give an example of a formula that is satisfiable but not a tautology.
solution: A → B is satisfiable (e.g. A=false) but not a tautology because for A=true,B=false it is false.

prompt: Convert ¬(A → (B ∨ C)) into CNF stepwise.
solution: A → (B ∨ C) ≡ ¬A ∨ B ∨ C. Negate: ¬(¬A ∨ B ∨ C) ≡ A ∧ ¬B ∧ ¬C. CNF: (A) ∧ (¬B) ∧ (¬C).

prompt: How many satisfying assignments does A ∨ B have (two vars)?
solution: A ∨ B is true for 3 of 4 valuations: (T,F), (F,T), (T,T) — so 3 satisfying assignments.

prompt: Explain exponential blow-up when converting to CNF (informal).
solution: Distributing ∨ over ∧ can duplicate subformulas; e.g. converting a sum-of-products with many nested alternations may produce exponentially many clauses in the worst case.

prompt: Define Negation Normal Form (NNF) with an example.
solution: NNF: only ∧, ∨ and literals, negation only on atoms. Example: ¬(A → B) → A ∧ ¬B is in NNF.

prompt: Explain equisatisfiable vs equivalent formulas.
solution: Equisatisfiable: both either have a satisfying assignment or not (may differ truth tables). Equivalent: same truth value for every valuation (identical truth table).

prompt: Give CNF for ¬(A ∧ (B → C)).
Define Skolemization (brief).
solution: Remove existential quantifiers by introducing fresh Skolem functions/constants; preserves satisfiability of first-order formulas after prenexing.

prompt: What is variable elimination in SAT preprocessing?
solution: Remove a variable by resolving all clauses containing it and replacing them with resolvents, possibly increasing clause count.

prompt: Explain backbone variables.
solution: Backbone: variables that have the same value in all satisfying assignments of a formula (forced values).

prompt: Define model counting (#SAT) briefly.
solution: #SAT: the problem of counting the number of satisfying assignments of a Boolean formula; generally #P-complete.

prompt: Describe clause learning (CDCL) in one sentence.
solution: When a conflict occurs, analyze the implication graph to derive a new clause (learned clause) that prevents repeating the same conflict.

prompt: What is the complexity of unit propagation per assignment?
solution: With watched literals, unit propagation runs amortized linear in the size of the formula per propagation step.

prompt: Define a clause database in modern SAT solvers.
solution: Repository of original and learned clauses maintained during search, used for propagation and conflict analysis.

prompt: Why are learned clauses useful?
solution: They prune the search space by preventing previously discovered conflicts and can accelerate future propagation.

prompt: Give a CNF for A ⊕ B (XOR).
solution: CNF: (A ∨ B) ∧ (¬A ∨ ¬B) enforces exactly-one-true for A and B.

prompt: Convert A → (B ∧ C) into CNF.
State the refutation method for entailment.
solution: Γ ⊨ φ iff Γ ∪ {¬φ} is unsatisfiable; prove entailment by showing unsatisfiability of the augmented set.

prompt: Explain resolution completeness succinctly.
solution: Resolution is refutation-complete for propositional logic: if a set of clauses is unsatisfiable, resolution derives the empty clause.

prompt: Define clause subsumption with an example.
solution: Clause C subsumes D if C's literals are a subset of D's; e.g., A ∨ B subsumes A ∨ B ∨ C and D can be removed.

prompt: What is blocked clause elimination (BCE)?
solution: Remove clauses that are blocked on a literal (resolving on that literal always produces tautologies), simplifying the formula without changing satisfiability.

prompt: Explain MaxSAT briefly.
solution: MaxSAT: optimization version of SAT that seeks an assignment maximizing the number of satisfied clauses (or minimizing unsatisfied clauses).

prompt: Give an example of a partial assignment and its effect.
solution: Partial assignment {A=true} simplifies clauses: A ∨ B becomes true (removed); ¬A ∨ C becomes C (unit), potentially triggering propagation.

prompt: What is preprocessing in SAT solving?
solution: Simplifications applied before search: eliminate pure literals, units, subsumed clauses, apply BCE, variable elimination to reduce formula size.

prompt: Define SMT (Satisfiability Modulo Theories).
solution: SMT generalizes SAT by checking satisfiability of formulas over background theories (e.g., arithmetic, arrays) combining Boolean search with theory solvers.

prompt: Explain literal polarity in formulas.
solution: Polarity of a literal occurrence is positive if the atom appears unnegated, negative if negated; polarity matters for simplifications and heuristics.

prompt: Define clause width.
solution: Width = number of literals in a clause; small-width clauses are generally easier for resolution and propagation.
